annotations.label: Linear map

anchor.snippet: a linear map (or linear mapping ) is a particular kind of function between vector spaces

annotations.note: `LinearMap` is the bundled structure of (semi)linear maps between modules, generalizing linear maps between vector spaces.

annotations.label: Linear map (formal definition)

anchor.snippet: A function

annotations.note: `IsLinearMap` packages the two axioms `map_add` and `map_smul` exactly as in the Wikipedia formal definition.

annotations.label: Preservation of linear combinations

anchor.snippet: Thus a linear map is one which preserves linear combinations

annotations.note: Linear maps preserve finite sums (via `map_sum`) and scalar multiples (`map_smul`), hence preserve linear combinations.

annotations.label: Linear map sends zero to zero

anchor.snippet: Denoting the zero elements of the vector spaces

annotations.note: `LinearMap.map_zero` (via the `ZeroHomClass` instance for `LinearMap`) states that any linear map sends 0 to 0.

annotations.label: Linear functional

anchor.snippet: viewed as a one-dimensional vector space over itself is called a linear functional

annotations.note: `Module.Dual R M = M →ₗ[R] R` is exactly the space of linear functionals.

annotations.label: Unique map of given form is linear

anchor.snippet: The unique map of the form

annotations.note: Mathlib has scaling-style linear maps like `LinearMap.smulRight`/`lsmul`, but no result phrased as 'unique map of the form x ↦ cx'.

annotations.label: Line through the origin

anchor.snippet: A prototypical example that gives linear maps their name

annotations.note: Multiplication-by-a-constant maps on ℝ exist via `LinearMap.lsmul`, but no Mathlib statement specifically about 'lines through the origin' as the prototypical example.

annotations.label: Homothety is linear

anchor.snippet: any homothety

annotations.note: `LinearMap.lsmul R M c : M →ₗ[R] M` is the homothety (scalar multiplication) packaged as a linear map.

annotations.label: Zero map is linear

anchor.snippet: The zero map

annotations.note: Mathlib gives `LinearMap` a `Zero` instance making the zero function a linear map.

annotations.label: Identity map is linear

anchor.snippet: The identity map on any module is a linear operator

annotations.note: `LinearMap.id : M →ₗ[R] M` is the identity packaged as a linear map.

annotations.label: Non-linear map (first)

anchor.snippet: For real numbers, the map

annotations.note: No specific example in Mathlib stating that some particular function (e.g., x ↦ x²) fails to be linear.

annotations.label: Affine but not linear

anchor.snippet: but is an affine transformation

annotations.note: `AffineMap` formalizes affine transformations, but no explicit Mathlib statement that x ↦ x+c is affine but not linear.

annotations.label: Matrix defines a linear map

anchor.snippet: real matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix.toLin'` converts a matrix to a linear map between coordinate spaces.

annotations.label: Isometry on real normed spaces

anchor.snippet: is an isometry between real normed spaces

annotations.note: `LinearIsometry` formalizes norm-preserving linear maps between normed spaces.

annotations.label: Differentiation is linear

anchor.snippet: Differentiation defines a linear map from the space of all differentiable functions

annotations.note: `Polynomial.derivative : R[X] →ₗ[R] R[X]` is differentiation as a linear map (on polynomials); analogous `fderiv`/`deriv` exist but are not bundled.

annotations.label: Definite integral is linear

anchor.snippet: A definite integral over some interval I is a linear map

annotations.note: Linearity of the definite interval integral is proved via `intervalIntegral.integral_add` and `integral_smul`, but the integral is not bundled as a single `LinearMap`.

annotations.label: Indefinite integral is linear

anchor.snippet: An indefinite integral (or antiderivative ) with a fixed integration starting point

annotations.note: No bundled linear-map version of the indefinite integral (antiderivative with fixed base point) in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear-maps-to-matrices is a linear isomorphism

anchor.snippet: are finite-dimensional vector spaces over a field F

annotations.note: `LinearMap.toMatrix` is the linear equivalence between linear maps and matrices given bases.

annotations.label: Expected value is linear

anchor.snippet: The expected value of a random variable is a linear function

annotations.note: Linearity of expectation follows from `integral_add`/`integral_smul`, but is not packaged as a single `LinearMap`.

annotations.label: Component scaling is linear

anchor.snippet: This function scales the

annotations.note: Component-scaling maps are special cases of `Pi.basisFun` combined with diagonal/`LinearMap.smulRight`-style constructions; no Mathlib lemma exactly named for the Wikipedia example.

annotations.label: Additive function example

anchor.snippet: is additive: It does not matter whether vectors are first added and then mapped

annotations.note: `LinearMap.map_add` (via `AddHomClass`) states that linear maps are additive.

annotations.label: Homogeneous function example

anchor.snippet: is homogeneous: It does not matter whether a vector is first scaled and then mapped

annotations.note: `LinearMap.map_smul` states that linear maps commute with scalar multiplication (homogeneity).

annotations.label: Linear isomorphism and endomorphism

anchor.snippet: If a linear map is a bijection then it is called a linear isomorphism

annotations.note: `LinearEquiv` and `Module.End` formalize linear isomorphisms and endomorphisms respectively.

annotations.label: Linear extension

anchor.snippet: Then a linear extension of

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Basis.constr` to extend a function on a basis to a linear map, but no general 'linear extension of a function on a subset' definition matching the Wikipedia notion.

annotations.label: Existence criterion for linear extension

anchor.snippet: can be extended to a linear map

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement of the existence criterion (consistency on linear-relations) for extending a partial function to a linear map.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of linear extension

anchor.snippet: If a linear extension of

annotations.note: `LinearMap.ext_on` / `Submodule.span_induction`-style results give uniqueness of linear maps agreeing on a spanning set, capturing the uniqueness claim.

annotations.label: Linearly independent set extension

anchor.snippet: is linearly independent then every function

annotations.note: `Basis.constr` lets any function on a basis be linearly extended; together with extension of a linearly independent set to a basis, this gives the Wikipedia statement.

annotations.label: Explicit linear extension example

anchor.snippet: can be linearly extended from the linearly independent set of vectors

annotations.note: No specific worked example of this form is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hahn–Banach dominated extension

anchor.snippet: the Hahn–Banach dominated extension theorem even guarantees

annotations.note: `exists_extension_of_le_sublinear` is the Hahn–Banach extension theorem for sublinear-dominated linear functionals.

annotations.label: Matrix representation of linear maps

anchor.snippet: every linear map from

annotations.note: `LinearMap.toMatrix` provides the bijection between linear maps and matrices for finite-dimensional spaces.

annotations.label: Rotation by 90 degrees counterclockwise

anchor.snippet: rotation by 90 degrees counterclockwise

annotations.note: Mathlib has 2D rotation matrices via `Matrix.planeConformalMatrix`, but no dedicated `rotation_by_90` example.

annotations.label: Rotation by angle θ

anchor.snippet: by an angle θ counterclockwise

annotations.note: `Matrix.planeConformalMatrix` gives the 2D rotation/conformal-scaling matrix; rotations are a special case.

annotations.label: Reflection through the x axis

anchor.snippet: reflection through the x axis

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Module.reflection` is the general involution-reflection construction; the specific x-axis reflection is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Reflection through the y axis

anchor.snippet: through the y axis

annotations.note: `Module.reflection` covers this in general; no x/y-axis-specific lemma.

annotations.label: Reflection through line at angle θ

anchor.snippet: through a line making an angle θ with the origin

annotations.note: Mathlib has the general `Module.reflection`, but no concrete 'reflection through a line at angle θ' example.

annotations.label: Scaling by 2

anchor.snippet: scaling by 2 in all directions

annotations.note: Scaling by a constant is `LinearMap.lsmul` applied to that scalar.

annotations.label: Horizontal shear mapping

anchor.snippet: horizontal shear mapping

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a shear mapping as a named linear map.

annotations.label: Skew of y axis

anchor.snippet: skew of the y axis by an angle θ

annotations.note: No skew/shear-by-angle named construction in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Squeeze mapping

anchor.snippet: squeeze mapping

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a squeeze mapping as a named transformation.

annotations.label: Projection onto y axis

anchor.snippet: projection onto the y axis

annotations.note: `LinearMap.proj` projects onto coordinate components of a Pi type; coordinate-axis projection is a special case.

annotations.label: Conformal linear transformation

anchor.snippet: then the linear map is a conformal linear transformation

annotations.note: `IsConformalMap` is the definition of a conformal linear map.

annotations.label: Composition of linear maps is linear

anchor.snippet: The composition of linear maps is linear

annotations.note: `LinearMap.comp` packages the composition of linear maps as a linear map.

annotations.label: Inverse of a linear map is linear

anchor.snippet: The inverse of a linear map, when defined, is again a linear map

annotations.note: `LinearEquiv.symm` exhibits the inverse of an invertible linear map as a linear map.

annotations.label: Pointwise sum of linear maps is linear

anchor.snippet: then so is their pointwise sum

annotations.note: Mathlib equips `M →ₛₗ[σ] N` with an `Add` instance, so the pointwise sum of linear maps is a linear map.

annotations.label: Scalar multiple of a linear map is linear

anchor.snippet: is an element of the ground field

annotations.note: Mathlib provides an `SMul` instance on `LinearMap`, making scalar multiples of linear maps linear.

annotations.label: Set of linear maps forms a vector space

anchor.snippet: of linear maps from

annotations.note: Mathlib gives `M →ₛₗ[σ] N` a `Module` instance, making `Hom(V,W)` a vector space.

annotations.label: Endomorphisms form an associative algebra

anchor.snippet: is an endomorphism of

annotations.note: `Module.End` is given `Semiring`/`Ring`/`Algebra` instances making it an associative algebra of endomorphisms.

annotations.label: Automorphism and automorphism group

anchor.snippet: An endomorphism of

annotations.note: `LinearMap.GeneralLinearGroup R M` is the group of linear automorphisms of `M`.

annotations.label: Endomorphism algebra isomorphic to matrices

anchor.snippet: has finite dimension

annotations.note: `LinearMap.toMatrixAlgEquiv` is the algebra isomorphism between `End M` and the matrix algebra given a basis.

annotations.label: Kernel and image

anchor.section: Kernel, image and the rank–nullity theorem
anchor.snippet: we define the kernel and the image or range of

annotations.note: `LinearMap.ker` and `LinearMap.range` define the kernel and image as submodules.

annotations.label: Rank–nullity theorem

anchor.section: Kernel, image and the rank–nullity theorem
anchor.snippet: The following dimension formula is known as the rank–nullity theorem

annotations.note: `LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker` is the rank–nullity formula `finrank(range) + finrank(ker) = finrank(V)`.

annotations.label: Rank and nullity

anchor.section: Kernel, image and the rank–nullity theorem
anchor.snippet: is also called the rank of

annotations.note: `LinearMap.rank` (and `finrank` of `ker`/`range`) define rank and nullity of a linear map.

annotations.label: Cokernel

anchor.snippet: A subtler invariant of a linear transformation

annotations.note: The cokernel is `M₂ ⧸ LinearMap.range f`, expressed using `Submodule` quotients; explicitly used in `Mathlib.Algebra.Module.Presentation.Cokernel`.

annotations.label: Cokernel exact sequence

anchor.snippet: Formally, one has the exact sequence

annotations.note: Exact-sequence formalism (e.g., `ShortComplex`/`cokernelSequence`) covers this in general categorical setting; no direct Wikipedia-style statement for modules.

annotations.label: Rank plus cokernel dimension

anchor.snippet: The dimension of the co-kernel and the dimension of the image

annotations.note: No explicit `finrank(range) + finrank(coker) = finrank(W)` lemma found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cokernel example f(x,y) = (0,y)

anchor.snippet: As a simple example, consider the map

annotations.note: No worked example of this specific cokernel computation in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinite-dimensional cokernel example

anchor.snippet: An example illustrating the infinite-dimensional case

annotations.note: No infinite-dimensional cokernel example of this type appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Index of a linear operator

anchor.snippet: For a linear operator with finite-dimensional kernel and co-kernel, one may define index

annotations.note: No general 'index = dim ker − dim coker' definition found for linear operators in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Index equals dim(V) − dim(W)

anchor.snippet: For a transformation between finite-dimensional vector spaces

annotations.note: No statement that the index of a linear map between finite-dimensional spaces is `dim V − dim W` in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Index as Euler characteristic

anchor.snippet: The index of an operator is precisely the Euler characteristic

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement connecting linear-operator index to the Euler characteristic of the associated complex.

annotations.label: Monomorphism (injective linear map)

anchor.snippet: is said to be injective or a monomorphism

annotations.note: Injectivity of a linear map is characterized by `LinearMap.ker_eq_bot` (kernel trivial iff injective).

annotations.label: Epimorphism (surjective linear map)

anchor.snippet: is said to be surjective or an epimorphism

annotations.note: Surjectivity is characterized by `LinearMap.range_eq_top`.

annotations.label: Isomorphism

anchor.snippet: is said to be an isomorphism if it is both left- and right-invertible

annotations.note: `LinearEquiv` is exactly the type of (two-sided invertible) linear isomorphisms.

annotations.label: Nilpotent, idempotent, scaling endomorphisms

anchor.snippet: is an endomorphism, then

annotations.note: `IsNilpotent` and `IsIdempotentElem` apply to `Module.End`; scalar-multiplication endomorphisms are `LinearMap.lsmul`.

annotations.label: Change of basis formula

anchor.snippet: Therefore, the matrix in the new basis

annotations.note: `basis_toMatrix_mul_linearMap_toMatrix_mul_basis_toMatrix` is the change-of-basis formula for a linear map's matrix.

annotations.label: Linear maps as (1,1) tensors

anchor.snippet: linear maps are said to be 1-co- 1-contra- variant objects

annotations.note: Mathlib has `dualTensorHomEquiv : M* ⊗ N ≃ₗ Hom(M,N)`, expressing linear maps as (1,1)-tensors; tensor-variance language itself is informal.

annotations.label: Continuity equals boundedness

anchor.snippet: A linear operator on a normed linear space is continuous if and only if it is bounded

annotations.note: Mathlib's `isLinearMap_and_continuous_iff_isBoundedLinearMap` (and related `LinearMap.continuous_of_bound`) state continuity equivalent to boundedness.

annotations.label: Differentiation is discontinuous

anchor.snippet: An example of an unbounded, hence discontinuous, linear transformation is differentiation

annotations.note: No explicit Mathlib example showing differentiation as an unbounded/discontinuous linear operator on a function space.
